plan: Examine the PCA clustering pattern and identify which samples group together.
Determine whether the groupings reflect treatment condition (T vs. C) or something else.
Name the effect suggested by the clustering.

Identify the appropriate method for visualizing diverse cell types in scRNA-seq data. --- ## Execution **Step 1 – Examine the clusters:** | Cluster | Members | Treatment mix? | |---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | C1, C3 | Control only | | 2 | T1, T3 | Treated only | | 3 | T4, T5, C6 | Mixed | | 4 | T2, C5, C6 | Mixed | | 5 | T6, C2, C4 | Mixed | **Step 2 – Determine what drives the separation:** The dominant groupings are **not** treatment vs. control. Instead, certain patients' samples co-cluster regardless of treatment (e.g., T6 groups with C2 and C4; T4/T5 group with C6). PC1 and PC2 are capturing variation that correlates with individual patients or processing batches, not the drug effect. **Step 3 – Name the effect:** This is characteristic of **batch effects** (and/or inter-patient / confounding effects) — technical or patient-level variation dominates over the biological signal of interest (treatment vs. placebo), producing clusters that don't align with the experimental groups. **Step 4 – Method for visualizing cell types:** For high-dimensional scRNA-seq data (100,000 cells × 9,000 genes), the standard approach to visualize and qualitatively assess distinct cell populations is **UMAP** (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection). After PCA-based dimensionality reduction and graph-based clustering (e.g., Leiden/Louvain), UMAP projects cells into 2D space where clusters corresponding to distinct cell types become visually apparent. Overlaying known marker genes on the UMAP allows qualitative annotation of each cluster (e.g., T cells, macrophages, tumor cells). --- FINAL ANSWER: The results suggest batch (confounding/inter-patient) effects; the method to visualize and qualitatively assess cell types is UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection).

trajectory.thought: The PCA scatter plot shows 5 distinct clusters: (C1, C3), (T1, T3), (T4, T5, C6), (T2, C5, C6), and (T6, C2, C4). Three of the five clusters contain mixed treated and control samples, meaning the separation is not driven by treatment condition but by patient/batch effects. The appropriate visualization method for diverse cell types in scRNA-seq data is UMAP.

The groupings clearly do not reflect treatment vs. control separation. Mixed clusters (T4/T5/C6, T2/C5/C6, T6/C2/C4) show treated and control samples co-clustering together. The separation is driven by patient-level or batch-level variation, not by drug treatment. This is characteristic of batch effects or confounding inter-patient effects.

The PCA clusters mix treated and control samples from the same or related patients/batches together, rather than separating by treatment. This indicates that the dominant source of variation is not the drug effect but rather patient-level or batch-level variation. This is the hallmark of **batch effects** (also called confounding or inter-patient effects) — where technical or biological variation between samples/patients dominates over the experimental variable of interest.

The plan is complete. The PCA scatter plot shows that samples cluster by patient/batch identity rather than treatment vs. control — this indicates batch effects (confounding/inter-patient effects). For visualizing diverse cell types in scRNA-seq data, UMAP is the standard method used after dimensionality reduction to reveal distinct cell populations in 2D space.
